rows.text: In eisa-playwright-e2e, FAQ E2E failures had three layered causes: (1) ClientSettings:HelpSupportApi was mis-seeded to the reporting API path in Services/Eisa.SqlDbServices/Migrations/20250401152121_ClientSettingNameUpdate.cs, which broke /faq loading until the DB/runtime value was corrected to /helpsupportapi/api/helpsupport/v1.0/; (2) eisa-ng/src/app/faq/faq-add-dialog/faq-add-dialog.component.ts initialized roles as null, which can trigger API 400s on create/update, so roles/tags should default to arrays; (3) faq.spec.ts assumed a visible Edit button after create and optional publish-confirm handling, but new answers start in edit mode and the publish dialog must be explicitly confirmed.

rows.text: In eisa-playwright-e2e, the client-settings protected-route gap came from app-routing loading AdminModule directly at `/admin/client-settings`, which rendered the module default route instead of the guarded `client-settings-table` child. Redirecting the parent path to `/admin/client-settings/client-settings-table` preserves the existing `id: 'client-settings'` guard. Validation also showed several first-test cold-start `page.goto(APP_URL)` timeouts/aborts across Playwright specs, so auth/anonymous bootstrap paths benefit from explicit root-navigation retries and less rigid landing assertions (`/`, `/home`, or `/login`).

rows.text: In eisa-playwright-e2e Playwright auth reuse is coupled to backend/session trust more than the plan implies: eisa-ng/e2e/playwright/fixtures/auth.ts treats cached storage state as fresh by file mtime only (5 minutes), reopens new contexts with ignoreHTTPSErrors, and CAC reuse still depends on per-context clientCertificates for KEYCLOAK_URL; meanwhile Makefile test-auth-preflight exports the Keycloak signing cert and explicitly restarts .NET services before auth tests, showing TLS trust propagation/backend token validation is a first-class prerequisite rather than a browser-only concern.

rows.text: GitHub Actions Windows failures in dotfiles Shell Tests were caused by claude/hooks/damage-control/ast_analyzer.py reapplying compiled bashToolPatterns without honoring YAML platform metadata. The main regex path already respected `platforms`/`exclude_platforms`, but AST extraction did not, so Linux-only Docker-down prompts still fired on Windows. Fix was to add shared platform-alias checks inside the AST pattern pass and cover it with tests in test_ast_analyzer.py.

entry.summary: GitHub Actions Windows failures in dotfiles Shell Tests were caused by claude/hooks/damage-control/ast_analyzer.py reapplying compiled bashToolPatterns without honoring YAML platform metadata. The main regex path already respected `platforms`/`exclude_platforms`, but AST extraction did not, so Linux-only Docker-down prompts still fired on Windows. Fix was to add shared platform-alias checks inside the AST pattern pass and cover it with tests in test_ast_analyzer.py.

rows.text: Local Keycloak startup instability in eisa-playwright-e2e was a readiness-budget problem, not evidence that the Keycloak 26.5.2 image is broken. Under full-stack cold starts, the container sometimes took well over 180s before opening port 8080 and beginning realm import, while faster runs became ready around 50-75s. The robust local-dev fix was to use Keycloak’s official `/health/ready` endpoint on the main HTTP port by setting `KC_HTTP_MANAGEMENT_HEALTH_ENABLED=false`, then widen the health budget (`start_period: 180s`, `retries: 24`). This validated across 3/3 full-stack cold starts.

entry.summary: Local Keycloak startup instability in eisa-playwright-e2e was a readiness-budget problem, not evidence that the Keycloak 26.5.2 image is broken. Under full-stack cold starts, the container sometimes took well over 180s before opening port 8080 and beginning realm import, while faster runs became ready around 50-75s. The robust local-dev fix was to use Keycloak’s official `/health/ready` endpoint on the main HTTP port by setting `KC_HTTP_MANAGEMENT_HEALTH_ENABLED=false`, then widen the health budget (`start_period: 180s`, `retries: 24`). This validated across 3/3 full-stack cold starts.

entry.finding: For Pi full-interaction trace plans, a sidecar JSONL cannot honestly promise one-file exact reconstruction if the same design also bounds inline payload size or relies on truncation/full-output-path spill metadata; exact reconstruction then requires deterministic sibling spill artifacts with coupled retention, or an explicit no-spill exemption for the payloads that must remain reconstructable from one file alone.

rows.text: copilot model filtering in refresh-models -- Copilot /models includes internal router IDs and hidden legacy entries. A robust refresh strategy is: parse only picker-enabled entries for newly discovered models, exclude internal accounts/* IDs, but preserve existing known models unless remote policy explicitly marks them disabled. This avoids polluting lists while not dropping known usable models like gpt-4.1/gpt-5.

rows.text: Infra incident 2026-04-20 auth-smoke was most strongly evidenced as a server-side TLS trust propagation failure, not pure Keycloak startup failure: Eisa.MvcWeb logged POST to https://keycloak:8443/.../token followed by AuthenticationException PartialChain. Local compose mounts cert files into .NET services but does not update the container trust store, while wait-for-keycloak.sh uses curl -sk and can mask exactly this class of failure. Secondary fragility comes from Compose using mostly service_started gating plus Playwright auth-state reuse by file mtime.

rows.text: Local suite completion required separating true backend-dependent AM-panel/user-management flows from stale-spec issues. Support Queries depends on IHttpSupportApp.GetQueries() and fails locally on the same Keycloak TLS PartialChain trust path as SCIM/MPS. entry.why_good: Local compose runs remain suite-acceptable by default, while trusted deployments can re-enable the same cases with E2E_REAL_BACKEND=1 and a dedicated one-file-at-a-time target list/state. This preserves current throughput without throwing away later backend-required coverage.

rows.text: Change the durable Playwright drive from a one-time snapshotted ordered target list to dynamic discovery plus a persisted completed-target set.

entry.decision: Change the durable Playwright drive from a one-time snapshotted ordered target list to dynamic discovery plus a persisted completed-target set.
entry.why_good: It preserves resume safety while fixing the exact class of miss we saw: newly added or previously omitted spec files can be picked up on later resumes without rerunning already completed files. rows.text: For Pi interaction tracing, start by logging only the delta missing from existing session persistence — preferably via session custom entries or a sidecar adjacent to the session file — before building a full mirrored trace pipeline.

entry.decision: For Pi interaction tracing, start by logging only the delta missing from existing session persistence — preferably via session custom entries or a sidecar adjacent to the session file — before building a full mirrored trace pipeline.
entry.why_good: Pi already stores most of the requested interaction record. A full mirror duplicates existing data, creates a redaction-vs-exactness conflict, and drags subagent/process-boundary and routing complexity into the MVP before the only clearly missing gap (provider request/response metadata and possibly mutated tool inputs) is proven insufficient.

rows.text: Do not promise single-file exact reconstruction when the same trace plan allows bounded inline payloads, truncation, or spill references; define the trace file as either containing the full redacted payload inline or pointing deterministically to a same-session spill artifact.

entry.decision: Do not promise single-file exact reconstruction when the same trace plan allows bounded inline payloads, truncation, or spill references; define the trace file as either containing the full redacted payload inline or pointing deterministically to a same-session spill artifact.
entry.why_good: This removes the internal contradiction without forcing unbounded JSONL growth. It preserves operator auditability while keeping sizing controls and spill paths available for large provider/tool payloads.

rows.text: For Playwright performance-plan validation, new make target names are not the key requirement. A dedicated perf harness is justified only to lock sample selection and persist timings; a dedicated Keycloak canary command is optional if existing repo-level targets already compose auth preflight plus a cache-cleared browser auth smoke. `bunx playwright test --list` is not acceptable auth/config validation because it exercises discovery only, not runtime login, TLS, storageState reuse, or service readiness.

entry.decision: For Playwright performance-plan validation, new make target names are not the key requirement. A dedicated perf harness is justified only to lock sample selection and persist timings; a dedicated Keycloak canary command is optional if existing repo-level targets already compose auth preflight plus a cache-cleared browser auth smoke. `bunx playwright test --list` is not acceptable auth/config validation because it exercises discovery only, not runtime login, TLS, storageState reuse, or service readiness.
entry.why_good: This avoids false confidence from command proliferation while still requiring the behaviors that actually catch regressions: auth-cache invalidation, browser-level login execution, preflight contract checks, deterministic sample timing, and seeded data for mutable flows.

rows.text: shell_tool_architecture

patterns: Tool.define() wraps init + execute with auto Zod validation and output truncation
spawn() uses detached:true only on non-Windows for process group killing
Shell.killTree: Windows=taskkill /f /t, Unix=SIGTERM process group then SIGKILL after 200ms
stdout and stderr merged into single output string with live metadata streaming
Timeout uses setTimeout + AbortSignal with cleanup on exit/error
PwshTool conditionally registered: Shell.pwsh() ? [PwshTool] : []
Tree-sitter WASM used for command parsing and permission pattern extraction
Output truncation: 2000 lines or 50KB, full output saved to file with hints
Plugin tools return simple string; registry applies truncation

rows.text: adversarial_plan_review

patterns: T1 verify commands that grep source files do not verify the artifact they are supposed to produce
rg exits 1 on no matches — verify commands in design-only phases will spuriously fail V gates
test -f on a spec file that may already exist is not a completion signal — check mtime or sentinel content
npx vitest run passes vacuously when no implementation changes are made — prose guard conditions are not enforced
MSYS2 /c/ paths fail in pwsh context — all verify commands need fallback forms
Manual review ACs with no structural checklist are trivially approvable — force structural requirements
Parallel tasks that share conceptual state (API design vs. boundary documentation) have hidden ordering dependencies
Sonnet validators reviewing Opus designs lack forcing function for subtle architectural gaps
rg on non-existent directory paths errors rather than returning empty — check path existence before V gate greps
Classifier signal injection risk in routing policy designs — always scope trust boundary in the design task

rows.text: architecture-plan completeness review

patterns: Success criteria that verify the plan file itself are circular — the plan file exists before any task runs, so test-f and rg checks against it pass trivially pre-execution
Architecture-only tasks that list 'Files' as sources to READ, not write, leave no prescribed output path — 'manual review' is unverifiable without a named artifact location

Parallel task scheduling that conflicts with a later cross-task integration check implies an undeclared dependency — catch this by checking if any validation gate requires output from both parallel tasks to be aligned
V1/V2 vitest guards hedged with 'if any implementation spike occurred' are non-deterministic — replace with unconditional run or explicit file-existence guard
rg patterns for function names not yet implemented will always fail in architecture-only waves — split pre-implementation boundary checks from post-implementation API surface checks
Wave failure recovery needs: owner, escalation trigger, max-iteration count, scope boundary — 'create a fix task' alone is insufficient for multi-agent loops

rows.text: scim_mps_e2e_dependencies

patterns: Profile self-edit tests explicitly skipped with SCIM dependency 500 error marker
CAC/mTLS authentication flows require real Keycloak → SCIM backend chain
Registration flows depend on SCIM user claims, identity mapping, and certificate validation
TLS PartialChain issues likely in Keycloak-to-backend SCIM service communication
Admin workflow UI can be safely isolated from real SCIM data dependencies
Form validation and navigation guards are frontend-only and safe to isolate
Certificate-based authentication cannot be meaningfully isolated without breaking core functionality

entry.pattern: Prompt-driven slash commands are high risk when raw user args are interpolated directly into skill text and then duplicated as free-form trailing args.
entry.risk: Prompt injection and instruction confusion; user-controlled path arguments become executable prompt content instead of inert data.
entry.mitigation: Validate/normalize args, reject control characters/newlines for path-only commands, and pass arguments in a clearly delimited data block or structured field without duplicate free-form injection.

entry.mitigation: Default to trusted/global agents, require explicit confirmation or allowlisting before using project-local agents, and make the chosen agent source visible in the final report.

rows.text: {"pattern":"Repo-local orchestrator plans that add persistent state, concurrency guards, and recovery actions are brittle when they omit atomic write/lock leasing, redaction rules for debug logs, and rollback semantics for partial recovery.","risk":"Stale locks, corrupted state.json/attempt logs, secret leakage in provider/tool diagnostics, and stack damage when restart sequences partially succeed or run concurrently with active jobs.","mitigation":"Use atomic temp-file+rename writes, lock files with owner+lease/heartbeat, redact/ACL debug artifacts, and make recovery actions mutually exclusive, precondition-checked, and resumable/rollback-capable."}

entry.pattern: Repo-local orchestrator plans that add persistent state, concurrency guards, and recovery actions are brittle when they omit atomic write/lock leasing, redaction rules for debug logs, and rollback semantics for partial recovery.
entry.risk: Stale locks, corrupted state.json/attempt logs, secret leakage in provider/tool diagnostics, and stack damage when restart sequences partially succeed or run concurrently with active jobs.
entry.mitigation: Use atomic temp-file+rename writes, lock files with owner+lease/heartbeat, redact/ACL debug artifacts, and make recovery actions mutually exclusive, precondition-checked, and resumable/rollback-capable.

rows.text: {"pattern":"Playwright performance plans that introduce auth reuse and selective parallelism are high risk when storageState cache files are shared on a host-mounted volume without per-worker/run namespacing, forced cold-path canaries, and explicit no-context-sharing rules.","risk":"Parallel workers or consecutive runs can read/write the same auth JSON, producing false-green canaries, role/session bleed, stale-session loops, and hard-to-reproduce Keycloak/TLS failures.","mitigation":"Namespace auth cache by worker/run, write atomically, exercise both cold and warm auth paths, clear cache on failure, and require fresh BrowserContext per test even when storageState is reused."}

entry.pattern: Playwright performance plans that introduce auth reuse and selective parallelism are high risk when storageState cache files are shared on a host-mounted volume without per-worker/run namespacing, forced cold-path canaries, and explicit no-context-sharing rules.
entry.risk: Parallel workers or consecutive runs can read/write the same auth JSON, producing false-green canaries, role/session bleed, stale-session loops, and hard-to-reproduce Keycloak/TLS failures.
entry.mitigation: Namespace auth cache by worker/run, write atomically, exercise both cold and warm auth paths, clear cache on failure, and require fresh BrowserContext per test even when storageState is reused.

rows.text: {"pattern":"Transcript/trace features that log exact provider payloads plus raw tool inputs/outputs become security liabilities when enablement is repo-controlled, redaction is key-name based, and parallel/subagent writes share a live append-only file.","risk":"Hidden system/context leakage, secrets embedded in free-form bash/write/edit payloads, durable pointers to temp-file full outputs, orphaned traces after session deletion, and corrupted or misleading audit logs under multi-process concurrency.","mitigation":"Keep enablement user-local and default-off, honor sensitive_repo as a hard disable or metadata-only mode, default-deny raw capture for free-form tool arguments/results, avoid persisting fullOutputPath, and use per-process traces with correlation IDs instead of shared live appends."}

entry.pattern: Transcript/trace features that log exact provider payloads plus raw tool inputs/outputs become security liabilities when enablement is repo-controlled, redaction is key-name based, and parallel/subagent writes share a live append-only file.
entry.risk: Hidden system/context leakage, secrets embedded in free-form bash/write/edit payloads, durable pointers to temp-file full outputs, orphaned traces after session deletion, and corrupted or misleading audit logs under multi-process concurrency.
entry.mitigation: Keep enablement user-local and default-off, honor sensitive_repo as a hard disable or metadata-only mode, default-deny raw capture for free-form tool arguments/results, avoid persisting fullOutputPath, and use per-process traces with correlation IDs instead of shared live appends.
